evaluation.manager_summary: Monica called seeking opioid treatment after 18 months of daily prescription pill use starting post-surgery. She's experiencing withdrawal symptoms when attempting to stop and has no prior treatment. Agent built strong rapport, conducted thorough screening, appropriately triaged urgency, and addressed self-pay concerns with concrete alternatives and a financial counselor callback within the hour. Compliance concern: agent overstepped into clinical territory multiple times by characterizing medical risk and predicting treatment modality rather than deferring to clinical team.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent acknowledged Monica by name immediately, validated her courage in reaching out within the first 15 seconds, normalized her experience ('very common path'), and explicitly removed blame ('doesn't make it your fault'). Tone was warm, unhurried, and matched the caller's emotional register throughout.
Caller explicitly stated self-pay and agent pivoted appropriately to financing options, sliding scale, and state programs. Per rubric, this should be scored under objection_handling instead.

Agent captured substance type (opioids/prescription pills), duration (18 months), frequency (daily), withdrawal symptoms, last attempt to stop, and co-occurring depression. All questions stayed in intake territory. One coaching opportunity: prior treatment history was asked ('Have you had any prior treatment for this?') but response was brief and agent moved on rather than exploring what 'handling it on my own' meant.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: When caller says 'handling it on my own,' consider a brief follow-up to clarify whether that means no professional help at all or if they've tried other interventions (AA, therapy, primary care support).

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Agent correctly identified withdrawal indicators (daily use, physical dependence, failed self-taper) and triaged appropriately by flagging for clinical intake, mentioning medically-supervised detox as a possibility, and setting a callback within the hour. No manufactured urgency, but appropriate gravity conveyed ('opioid withdrawal...really does need professional support').
Caller is the patient herself, confirmed early in the call ('I'm calling about getting into a program. I've been dealing with opioid use').

Caller expressed cost concern as self-pay. Agent acknowledged immediately ('I don't want cost to be what stands between you and getting help'), offered three concrete alternatives (sliding scale, CareCredit financing, state programs), asked clarifying questions about financial situation to personalize recommendations, and set clear next step with financial counselor. No pressure, dismissiveness, or defensive language.

Agent set two specific next steps: (1) financial counselor callback within the hour, and (2) clinical intake team readiness. Timing was explicit ('within the hour'), ownership was clear (counselor and clinical team), and caller confirmed availability. No ambiguity.

Multiple scope overreach violations: (1) 'Opioid withdrawal can be medically serious' — clinical characterization of medical risk that belongs to a clinician, not intake; (2) 'Our clinical team will likely recommend starting with MAT' — pre-assessing treatment modality before clinical evaluation; (3) 'that's what dual diagnosis care looks like' — educational-clinical framing without deferral. Pattern of stepping into clinical territory rather than deferring consistently.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Avoid clinical characterizations like 'medically serious' or predicting treatment recommendations ('will likely recommend MAT'). Instead, defer: 'Our clinical team will assess what level of support is safest for you' keeps you in the intake lane.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Thank you for reaching out, Monica. That takes courage and I'm glad you called.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: That's a very common path and it doesn't make it your fault. You got trapped by something that started as medical treatment.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: opioid withdrawal with the symptoms you're describing really does need professional support — you don't have to white-knuckle this.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: When you say sick — are you talking about withdrawal symptoms? Sweating, muscle aches, nausea?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Monica, you mentioned withdrawal symptoms when you try to cut down — how recently did you last try to stop?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Are there any other health concerns, mental health or otherwise, that we should know about?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Opioid withdrawal can be medically serious and our clinical team will want to assess whether medically-supervised detox is the right first step

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm going to flag you for a call from our financial counsellor within the hour, and I'll also get our clinical intake team ready

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: opioid withdrawal with the symptoms you're describing really does need professional support

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Self-pay is something we work with regularly and I don't want cost to be what stands between you and getting help.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: We have a sliding-scale fee structure based on income, and we also work with a financing partner — CareCredit — that offers low-interest payment plans.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: let me connect you with our financial counsellor who can walk through the actual numbers with you...You'll have a clear picture of what you'd actually owe before you commit

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm going to flag you for a call from our financial counsellor within the hour, and I'll also get our clinical intake team ready with the information you've shared today.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Can you take a call in the next sixty minutes?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: You'll hear from us within the hour.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Opioid withdrawal can be medically serious and our clinical team will want to assess whether medically-supervised detox is the right first step

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Our clinical team will likely recommend starting with MAT — medication-assisted treatment — which uses medications like buprenorphine to manage withdrawal safely.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: It's likely both, and we treat them together — that's what dual diagnosis care looks like.
